verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच
सन्न्यासं कर्मणां कृष्ण पुनर्योगं च शंससि ।
यच्छ्रेय एतयोरेकं तन्मे ब्रूहि सुनिश्चितम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
arjuna uvāca
sannyāsaṁ karmaṇāṁ kṛṣṇa punar yogaṁ ca śaṁsasi |
yac chreya etayor ekaṁ tan me brūhi su-niścitam || 1 ||
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara reads 5.1 as Arjuna voicing a doubt born of apparently contradictory teaching: in earlier chapters Krishna has praised both renunciation of action (sannyāsa of karmas, in chapter 2 and 3.4) and the obligatory performance of action (karma-yoga, 3.8 etc.). Arjuna asks for one of the two to be definitely commended as the better, given they look opposed. Shankara emphasises that the question is sincere — it is not a hostile challenge but a request from the student who has earned the right to a verdict.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja groups 5.1 as Arjuna's objection: 'You praise the renunciation of action, i.e., jñāna-yoga, at one time, and karma-yoga at another.' He notes Arjuna's earlier confusion (in 3.1-3.2) and his current pressing question — these two are means independent of each other in Vishishtadvaita's reading; Arjuna is asking which is the *uniquely* better path for him, not whether both are valid in principle.
verses.literal_meaning: Arjuna said: O Krishna, you praise the renunciation of actions, and again you praise yoga (karma-yoga). Tell me decisively: which one of these two is more beneficial?
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Arjuna opens chapter 5 with a sharp question. Having heard Krishna praise both sannyāsa (in chapter 3 verses on the path of jñāna-niṣṭhā) and karma-yoga (in chapter 3.8 and elsewhere), he experiences the teaching as containing two recommendations that can look mutually exclusive, and asks for a decisive verdict. The verse opens the chapter dialectically — the student presses the teacher for one answer over the other, having earned the right to do so by chapter 4's accumulated trust.

positions.rendering: work in devotion / devotional service (Gaudiya emphasis)
karma-yoga, the obligatory performance of scripturally enjoined action
karma-yoga as one of two independent means alongside jñāna-yoga

disagreements.explanation: Prabhupada's 'work in devotion' reflects the Gaudiya rendering of yoga as bhakti-tinged karma-yoga; Shankara reads yoga here narrowly as obligatory karma performed without attachment (yoga as viparyaya of sannyāsa); Ramanuja reads it as karma-yoga, one of two soteriological paths, with the second being jñāna-yoga (which he then identifies with sannyāsa here).

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
सन्न्यासः कर्मयोगश्च निःश्रेयसकरावुभौ ।
तयोस्तु कर्मसन्न्यासात्कर्मयोगो विशिष्यते ॥ २ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
sannyāsaḥ karma-yogaś ca niḥśreyasa-karāv ubhau |
tayos tu karma-sannyāsāt karma-yogo viśiṣyate || 2 ||

anvaya.meaning: lead to the supreme good / liberation
is superior / is distinguished as better

verses.shankara_summary: Shankara stresses the two-half structure: both sannyāsa and karma-yoga lead to liberation (because both can give rise to Knowledge); however, of these two, karma-yoga is to be regarded as superior to the renunciation of action. He glosses 'tu' and 'viśiṣyate' carefully — the preference is structural, not absolute. Karma-yoga is praised here in the context of the unenlightened person who has not yet realized non-doership; for such a person, karma-yoga is the appropriate practice, while pure sannyāsa belongs to the one already established in jñāna-niṣṭhā.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja: even granting that some are competent for the practice of jñāna-yoga (which he equates with sannyāsa here) exclusively, both sannyāsa and karma-yoga are independent means leading to the supreme good. Of these two, karma-yoga is the superior. This is a distinct claim from Shankara's: for Ramanuja the two are independent, not stages of a single ladder, and the preference of karma-yoga is direct.
verses.literal_meaning: The Supreme Lord said: Both renunciation of action and karma-yoga lead to the supreme good. Of these two, however, karma-yoga is superior to the renunciation of action.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna gives the chapter's thesis-verdict in two halves. First: both paths produce niḥśreyas (the supreme good, liberation). Second: of the two, karma-yoga is to be regarded as superior. The two halves are load-bearing and must be preserved together. This is not a flat 'either is fine' equivalence; nor is it 'sannyāsa is wrong'. It is: equivalence-of-destination plus structural-preference-in-practice.

positions.rendering: is better (in practice)
is superior, but staged: karma-yoga for the unenlightened, sannyāsa for the established jñānin
is the superior independent means

disagreements.explanation: Shankara reads the preference as staged (karma-yoga is superior *for the still-becoming aspirant*); Ramanuja reads the preference as direct (karma-yoga is superior simpliciter, given that both are valid independent means). Prabhupada flattens this into 'better' without distinguishing the staged-vs-direct disagreement.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
ज्ञेयः स नित्यसन्न्यासी यो न द्वेष्टि न काङ्क्षति ।
निर्द्वन्द्वो हि महाबाहो सुखं बन्धात्प्रमुच्यते ॥ ३ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
jñeyaḥ sa nitya-sannyāsī yo na dveṣṭi na kāṅkṣati |
nirdvandvo hi mahā-bāho sukhaṁ bandhāt pramucyate || 3 ||
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: the karma-yogin who neither hates nor craves is to be known as a nitya-sannyāsī — the constant renunciate. Such a person, free of dualities (heat-cold, pleasure-pain, success-failure), is easily released from bondage. Shankara emphasises that nitya-sannyāsa is an inner state available *within* karma-yoga; one need not externally renounce action to inhabit it.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja: the karma-yogin who, satisfied with the experience of the self implied in karma-yoga, neither desires anything different nor hates anything, who endures the pairs of opposites, is to be understood as ever given to renunciation. This anchors nitya-sannyāsa to the karma-yogin's inner equanimity, not to external renunciation.
verses.literal_meaning: He is to be known as a constant renunciate who neither hates nor longs; free of dualities, O mighty-armed one, he is easily liberated from bondage.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna gives a precise definition of nitya-sannyāsa: it is not external giving-up of action but the inner state of the one without dveṣa (aversion) and ākāṅkṣā (longing). Such a person is nirdvandva — beyond the play of opposites — and is *easily* (sukham) liberated from bondage. The verse establishes that the karma-yogin can already be a sannyāsī in the sense that matters.

positions.rendering: always renounced (a sthitaprajña-style description)
the karma-yogin who has inwardly renounced via equanimity, equivalent in inner state to the formal sannyāsī
the karma-yogin satisfied with self-experience, ever given to renunciation in the relevant sense

disagreements.explanation: All three converge on the inner-state reading. The disagreement is in scope: Shankara reads nitya-sannyāsī as identifying the equivalence of inner-state karma-yogin with the formal sannyāsī; Ramanuja makes the karma-yogin's satisfaction with self-experience the criterion; Prabhupada renders idiomatically without taking a strong sectarian position.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
साङ्ख्ययोगौ पृथग्बालाः प्रवदन्ति न पण्डिताः ।
एकमप्यास्थितः सम्यगुभयोर्विन्दते फलम् ॥ ४ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
sāṅkhya-yogau pṛthag bālāḥ pravadanti na paṇḍitāḥ |
ekam apy āsthitaḥ samyag ubhayor vindate phalam || 4 ||
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: only the immature (bāla) speak of sāṅkhya (the path of jñāna-yoga) and yoga (karma-yoga) as different. The wise (paṇḍita) know they are not different in essence — both lead to the same fruit. The one who has rightly established themselves in even one of the two obtains the fruit of both. Shankara emphasises that the convergence is at the level of fruit, not in the immediate practice.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja: only the unlearned speak of these as different; the learned do not. The one rightly established in even one of these obtains the fruit of both. For Ramanuja the unity is more direct because karma-yoga properly performed *includes* the elements of jñāna; the two paths are not radically different but two articulations of the same soteriological project.
verses.literal_meaning: Only the unwise speak of sāṅkhya and yoga as different, not the wise. The one rightly established in even one of these obtains the fruit of both.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna refuses the binary at the level of the wise. Sāṅkhya (the path of analytical knowledge) and yoga (the path of disciplined action) are not different in their fruit. The one rightly established in either path obtains what the other path offers. This is the equivalence claim the chapter has been building since 5.2.

positions.rendering: devotional service [karma-yoga] and analytical study [Sāṅkhya]
jñāna-yoga and karma-yoga, two paths converging in their fruit
two articulations of the same soteriological project, jñāna-yoga and karma-yoga

disagreements.explanation: All readings converge on identifying sāṅkhya here with the path of knowledge and yoga with the path of action. The disagreement is in how unified the two are: Shankara sees them as distinct paths converging in fruit; Ramanuja sees them as more deeply unified (karma-yoga properly understood includes jñāna).

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
यत्साङ्ख्यैः प्राप्यते स्थानं तद्योगैरपि गम्यते ।
एकं साङ्ख्यं च योगं च यः पश्यति स पश्यति ॥ ५ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
yat sāṅkhyaiḥ prāpyate sthānaṁ tad yogair api gamyate |
ekaṁ sāṅkhyaṁ ca yogaṁ ca yaḥ paśyati sa paśyati || 5 ||
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: the place (sthāna, i.e., liberation) reached by the sāṅkhyas is the same place reached by the yogis. The one who sees sāṅkhya and yoga as one (in their fruit) sees rightly. Shankara's reading preserves the convergence at the destination while distinguishing the practices.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja: the state attained by the followers of jñāna-yoga is also reached by the followers of karma-yoga. The one who sees both paths as one sees correctly. For Ramanuja the unity is in the soteriological function — both are means to the self's release.
verses.literal_meaning: That state which is reached by the sāṅkhyas is reached also by the yogis. The one who sees sāṅkhya and yoga as one — he sees.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna repeats and strengthens 5.4. The sthāna (state, place) reached by jñāna-yogis is the same sthāna reached by karma-yogis. To see them as one is to see correctly. The verse closes the equivalence argument that began at 5.2.

disagreements.word: ekaṁ sāṅkhyaṁ ca yogaṁ ca yaḥ paśyati

positions.rendering: sees analytical study and devotional service to be on the same level
sees the two paths as converging in their fruit (oneness of destination)
sees the two paths as one in soteriological function

disagreements.explanation: All converge on 'oneness'. Shankara confines the oneness to fruit; Ramanuja extends it to function; Prabhupada renders idiomatically as 'same level'. None of the three flatten the two paths into mere identity at the level of practice — the practices remain distinct.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
सन्न्यासस्तु महाबाहो दुःखमाप्तुमयोगतः ।
योगयुक्तो मुनिर्ब्रह्म नचिरेणाधिगच्छति ॥ ६ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
sannyāsas tu mahā-bāho duḥkham āptum ayogataḥ |
yoga-yukto munir brahma na cireṇādhigacchati || 6 ||

anvaya.meaning: joined to yoga / engaged in karma-yoga

verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: real sannyāsa is hard to attain without yoga (karma-yoga). The muni (one who meditates on the real nature of the Self) who is yoga-yukta — equipped with karma-yoga as a preparatory discipline — attains brahman without delay. Shankara emphasises the staged reading: karma-yoga is the necessary preparation; sannyāsa proper is the consequent state of the established jñānin.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja: renunciation, i.e., jñāna-yoga, cannot be attained without karma-yoga. A person following karma-yoga, being himself a muni (one engaged in contemplation of the self), reaches brahman with ease — i.e., attains the self soon. Ramanuja's reading: karma-yoga is the means by which one becomes the muni who attains brahman; jñāna-yoga without karma-yoga is impossible.
verses.literal_meaning: But, O mighty-armed one, sannyāsa is hard to attain without yoga; the muni joined to yoga reaches brahman without delay.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names the structural preference: pure sannyāsa is hard to reach without first cultivating karma-yoga. The yoga-yukta muni attains brahman without delay. The verse explains why karma-yoga is preferable in practice (5.2): it is the discipline that makes sannyāsa attainable. Renunciation as flight from action — without first cultivating equanimity in action — does not produce the inner state sannyāsa names.

positions.rendering: without devotional service
without karma-yoga as preparatory discipline
without karma-yoga as means

disagreements.explanation: All three converge on yoga = karma-yoga (the disciplined practice of action). The disagreement is in the relationship: Shankara reads karma-yoga as preparatory and sannyāsa as consequent; Ramanuja reads karma-yoga as the means by which one becomes the muni who attains brahman; Prabhupada idiomatically renders yoga as 'devotional service'.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
योगयुक्तो विशुद्धात्मा विजितात्मा जितेन्द्रियः ।
सर्वभूतात्मभूतात्मा कुर्वन्नपि न लिप्यते ॥ ७ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
yoga-yukto viśuddhātmā vijitātmā jitendriyaḥ |
sarva-bhūtātma-bhūtātmā kurvann api na lipyate || 7 ||

anvaya.meaning: whose Self is the Self of all beings

verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: the yoga-yukta is purified in the antaḥkaraṇa, has conquered the mind (vijitātmā), has subdued the senses, and recognises the Self as the Self of all beings. Such a one, though acting, is not bound by action. Shankara's emphasis: the four qualifications (yoga-yukta, viśuddhātmā, vijitātmā, jitendriya) plus the recognition of the universal Self produce the not-bound-by-action state. He notes this is an Advaita anchor — the Self of the yogin is identical with the Self of all beings.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja: the karma-yogin whose mind is purified, who has conquered the inner sense, whose external senses are subdued, who looks upon the self of all beings as akin to his own self (in being a knower of the same form), even when acting is not entangled by action. For Ramanuja the 'sarva-bhūtātma-bhūtātmā' is read as similarity of nature among individual selves, not Advaita identity.
verses.literal_meaning: The one joined to yoga, with self purified, mind conquered, senses controlled, whose self is the self of all beings — though acting, is not entangled.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna describes the yogi who acts without bondage. Four qualifications: joined-to-yoga, purified, mind-conquered, sense-controlled. Plus the universal-Self recognition. Such a one acts (kurvan api) but is not stained (na lipyate). The verse pre-figures 18.66 and is the close cousin of 4.20 (ever-content, takes shelter in nothing, engaged-in-action does not act).

positions.rendering: dear to everyone, and everyone is dear to him (relational)
whose Self is identical with the Self of all beings (Advaita identity)
whose self looks upon the selves of all beings as akin to his own (similarity of nature)

disagreements.explanation: Major sectarian disagreement. Shankara reads identity (one universal Self in all beings); Ramanuja reads similarity (the karma-yogin sees other individual selves as akin to his own); Prabhupada renders relationally as universal love. The engineering analog must respect this disagreement and not flatten it.

verses.sanskrit_iast:
naiva kiñcit karomīti yukto manyeta tattva-vit |
paśyañ śṛṇvan spṛśañ jighrann aśnan gacchan svapan śvasan || 8 ||
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: the tattva-vit (knower of truth), absorbed in the Self, should think 'I do nothing at all' — even while seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, going, sleeping, and breathing. Shankara emphasises this is the realised non-doership of the established jñānin: the senses operate, the body acts, but the Self does not act. This is the cessation of the false agency-attribution to the Self.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja groups 5.8 and 5.9 together: the one who knows the truth concerning the self should reflect that the jñānendriyas, karmendriyas, and the prāṇas are occupied with their own respective objects. He should know 'I do not do anything at all' — meaning, the self (which is distinct from these instruments) does not do; the instruments do. Ramanuja's reading is dualistic: the self is the experiencer/witness, the body-mind-prāṇa complex is the doer.
verses.literal_meaning: The yogi knower-of-truth should think 'I do nothing at all' — while seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, going, sleeping, and breathing.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names the doctrine of non-doership for the seer-of-truth. Across the most basic activities of embodied life — perception, locomotion, alimentation, respiration — the tattva-vit recognises that the senses and body are operating; the Self does not. This is structural-perceptual, not vocational. The verse continues into 5.9 and is read as a pair with it.

positions.rendering: always knows within himself that he actually does nothing at all (devotional consciousness)
Advaita: the Self does not act; the body-senses act; the jñānin recognises this and disowns false agency
Vishishtadvaita: the self is distinct from body-mind-prāṇa instruments; the instruments do, the self does not

disagreements.explanation: Major doctrinal divergence. Shankara: agency-of-the-Self is illusory; only senses-and-body act. Ramanuja: agency-of-the-jīva is real but pertains to body-mind-prāṇa, not to the self proper. Prabhupada renders devotionally. All three preserve the structural claim: 'I' (in the proper sense) do nothing while seeing/hearing/etc.

verses.sanskrit_iast:
pralapan visṛjan gṛhṇann unmiṣan nimiṣann api |
indriyāṇīndriyārtheṣu vartanta iti dhārayan || 9 ||
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: continuing 5.8, while talking, releasing, grasping, opening and closing the eyes — the realised yogi maintains the recognition that the senses are operating among sense-objects, not the Self. The repetition of bodily/sensory verbs across 5.8-5.9 is exhaustive: the verse covers perception, action, locomotion, alimentation, respiration, speech, elimination, reception, and the finest motor activity (opening/closing eyes), to make clear the recognition is comprehensive.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja groups 5.8-5.9 together: while talking, releasing, grasping, opening and closing the eyes, the knower-of-truth holds in mind that the senses operate among sense-objects. The self does not. The dualistic reading: the senses (jñānendriyas + karmendriyas) and prāṇas operate; the self witnesses.
verses.literal_meaning: While talking, releasing, grasping, opening and closing the eyes also — knowing that 'the senses operate among sense-objects'.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: 5.8-5.9 form a single doctrinal unit. The seer-of-truth, across all the activities of embodied existence, holds in mind that 'the senses operate among sense-objects' — the work proceeds via the body-sense-mind apparatus; the Self does not act. This is the Gita's most concentrated statement of the non-doership doctrine.

positions.rendering: always knows that only the material senses are engaged with their objects and that he is aloof from them
the realised jñānin holds the structural recognition that senses act, Self does not (Advaita non-doership)
the knower of truth holds in mind that the senses operate among objects (dualistic, self distinct from senses)

disagreements.explanation: All converge on the structural claim. The disagreement is metaphysical: Shankara's Advaita reads the Self as wholly non-acting; Ramanuja's Vishishtadvaita reads the self as the experiencer-witness distinct from the body-sense-prāṇa complex. Prabhupada emphasises the devotional aloofness.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
ब्रह्मण्याधाय कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा करोति यः ।
लिप्यते न स पापेन पद्मपत्रमिवाम्भसा ॥ १० ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
brahmaṇy ādhāya karmāṇi saṅgaṁ tyaktvā karoti yaḥ |
lipyate na sa pāpena padma-patram ivāmbhasā || 10 ||
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: the karma-yogin who surrenders all actions to brahman (with the attitude 'I act for Him as a servant for the master') and abandons attachment is not stained by sin — as the lotus leaf is not stained by water. Shankara emphasises that this is the karma-yogin's path: surrender of agency to brahman plus abandonment of attachment yields non-staining.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja: the one who places action in brahman (i.e., dedicates action to the Lord), abandons attachment, and acts, is not stained by pāpa as the lotus leaf is not stained by water. For Ramanuja brahmaṇi ādhāya is read as dedication-to-the-Lord; the karma-yogin's action becomes worship and does not bind.
verses.literal_meaning: The one who, having placed actions in brahman and abandoned attachment, acts — he is not stained by sin, as the lotus leaf is not stained by water.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna gives the lotus-leaf metaphor for the karma-yogin's relation to action. Two prerequisites: placing action in brahman (offering / surrender) and abandoning saṅga (attachment). With both, the karma-yogin acts but is not stained by sin. The metaphor is structural: the lotus leaf sits in water — full surface contact — and is not penetrated by it. This is the mature form of the action-without-bondage thread (cf. 3.9, 4.20, 5.7).

positions.rendering: surrendering the results unto the Supreme Lord
surrendering action to brahman with the servant-master attitude
dedicating action to the Lord (action becomes worship)

disagreements.explanation: Sectarian reading varies. Shankara reads brahman impersonally (the absolute) but allows the servant-master devotional attitude; Ramanuja reads the Lord (Vishnu/Narayana) personally; Prabhupada renders devotionally. All preserve the structural claim: the orientation of action toward brahman (or the Lord) and the abandonment of attachment together produce non-staining.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
कायेन मनसा बुद्ध्या केवलैरिन्द्रियैरपि ।
योगिनः कर्म कुर्वन्ति सङ्गं त्यक्त्वात्मशुद्धये ॥ ११ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
kāyena manasā buddhyā kevalair indriyair api |
yoginaḥ karma kurvanti saṅgaṁ tyaktvātma-śuddhaye || 11 ||
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: the yogis (karma-yogins, here distinct from the established jñānin of 5.8-5.9) act with body, mind, intellect, and even with the senses alone — having abandoned attachment, for the purification of the self (antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi). The verse names the karma-yogin's full-faculty engagement: every layer of the apparatus is engaged, none withheld.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja: karma-yogins perform action with body, mind, intellect, and the senses, having abandoned the attachment that consists in identifying these instruments with the self. The action is for the purification of the self — meaning, for the loosening of the karma-binding that obscures self-knowledge.
verses.literal_meaning: Yogis perform action with the body, the mind, the intellect, and even with mere senses, having abandoned attachment, for the purification of the self.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names what the karma-yogin engages: kāya (body), manas (mind), buddhi (intellect), and even kevalaiḥ indriyaiḥ (the senses alone). All faculties active. The sole withholding is saṅga (attachment). The purpose is ātma-śuddhi (self-purification). The verse rejects partial engagement as a misreading of karma-yoga: every layer is in the work; only attachment is not.

positions.rendering: purification (of the soul)
antaḥkaraṇa-śuddhi (purification of the inner instrument as preparation for jñāna)
loosening of karma-binding that obscures self-knowledge

disagreements.explanation: All three preserve self-purification as karma-yoga's purpose. Shankara reads it as preparation for jñāna; Ramanuja reads it as karma-loosening preparatory to self-realization; Prabhupada renders devotionally.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
युक्तः कर्मफलं त्यक्त्वा शान्तिमाप्नोति नैष्ठिकीम् ।
अयुक्तः कामकारेण फले सक्तो निबध्यते ॥ १२ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
yuktaḥ karma-phalaṁ tyaktvā śāntim āpnoti naiṣṭhikīm |
ayuktaḥ kāma-kāreṇa phale sakto nibadhyate || 12 ||
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: the yukta (the karma-yogin) abandons the fruit of action and obtains naiṣṭhikī (final, lasting) peace. The ayukta (the non-karma-yogin) is impelled by desire, attached to the fruit, and is bound. The verse anchors the chapter to 2.47-2.48: detachment from fruit produces peace; attachment to fruit produces bondage.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja: the disciplined one, having abandoned attachment to the fruit of action, obtains the firm peace consisting of self-experience. The undisciplined one, driven by desire, attached to the fruit, is bound by karma. Ramanuja's reading anchors śānti to the experience of the self (ātma-anubhava).
verses.literal_meaning: The disciplined one, having abandoned the fruit of action, obtains lasting peace; the undisciplined one, driven by desire, attached to the fruit, is bound.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna sets up a clean structural contrast. The yukta abandons phala (fruit) and gains naiṣṭhikī śānti (lasting peace). The ayukta, impelled by kāma (desire), attached to phala, is bound (nibadhyate). The verse builds directly on 2.47-2.48 and gives the chapter's clearest operational test: did detachment-from-fruit produce peace?

positions.rendering: unadulterated peace (because results offered to Me)
final, lasting peace (mokṣa-ānanda-anchored)
firm peace consisting of self-experience

disagreements.explanation: All converge on lasting/firm peace. The disagreement is the metaphysical anchor: Shankara reads it as the peace of mokṣa; Ramanuja anchors it to ātma-anubhava; Prabhupada to the devotional surrender of fruit.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
सर्वकर्माणि मनसा सन्न्यस्यास्ते सुखं वशी ।
नवद्वारे पुरे देही नैव कुर्वन्न कारयन् ॥ १३ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
sarva-karmāṇi manasā sannyasyāste sukhaṁ vaśī |
nava-dvāre pure dehī naiva kurvan na kārayan || 13 ||

anvaya.meaning: in the city of nine gates

verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: the self-controlled (vaśī) embodied one (dehī), having mentally renounced (manasā sannyasya) all actions through discriminating wisdom — i.e., having seen inaction in action — dwells happily in the city of nine gates (the body of nine orifices: two eyes, two ears, two nostrils, mouth, anus, genital), neither acting nor causing-others-to-act. Shankara emphasises this is the established jñānin's state: the body acts, the senses act, but the embodied Self neither acts nor causes.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja: the self-controlled embodied self renounces all actions to the city of nine gates — i.e., the body with its sensory and motor functions which are nine in number. He discriminates that all actions are due to the conjunction of the self with the body, which is rooted in previous karma, and dwells happily, neither acting nor causing others to act. For Ramanuja, the renunciation is the recognition that action belongs to the body, not to the self.
verses.literal_meaning: The self-controlled embodied one, having mentally renounced all actions, dwells happily in the city of nine gates, neither acting nor causing action.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna gives the metaphor of the body as the city of nine gates (nava-dvāra-pure: two eyes, two ears, two nostrils, mouth, anus, genital). The vaśī (self-controlled) dehī (embodied) mentally renounces all action and dwells happily within this city, neither acting nor causing others to act. The verse anchors the inner-renunciation reading: bodily action continues; the embodied Self has renounced it at the level of mind.

positions.rendering: in the city of nine gates [the material body]
Advaita: the body of nine orifices is the field; the embodied Self dwells in it without acting
Vishishtadvaita: the embodied self renounces all actions to the body, recognising they belong to body-not-self

disagreements.explanation: Both classical readings preserve the body=nine-gated-city image. Shankara emphasises the embodied Self's non-action; Ramanuja emphasises the renunciation-of-action-to-the-body via discrimination. The metaphysical scope (the nature of the embodied Self vs. the body) is sectarian; the structural image is shared.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
न कर्तृत्वं न कर्माणि लोकस्य सृजति प्रभुः ।
न कर्मफलसंयोगं स्वभावस्तु प्रवर्तते ॥ १४ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
na kartṛtvaṁ na karmāṇi lokasya sṛjati prabhuḥ |
na karma-phala-saṁyogaṁ svabhāvas tu pravartate || 14 ||

anvaya.meaning: nor union with the fruits of action

verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: the prabhu (the Self/Ātman, the master of the body-mind) does not create agency for the world (does not say 'do this'), nor does it create actions (objects such as chariots, pots), nor union-with-the-fruits-of-action. Rather, svabhāva (own-nature, the projection of māyā / avidyā) operates. Shankara's Advaita reading: the Self is not the agent; agency is misattributed to the Self because of avidyā.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja: the prabhu (the jīva, the master of the body, who is established in its own essential nature, not subject to karma) does not bring about agency, action, or union-with-fruits-of-action for the world (the field of body-and-its-environment). Rather, svabhāva — i.e., the conjunction of the self with prakṛti rooted in beginningless karma — operates. Ramanuja's reading: the self proper does not create agency; the karmically-conditioned conjunction does.
verses.literal_meaning: The Lord/master does not create agency, nor actions, nor union with the fruits of action, for the world. Rather, own-nature operates.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna makes a metaphysical claim about agency. The prabhu (Lord / master / embodied Self, depending on tradition) does not create kartṛtva (agency), karma (action), or karma-phala-saṁyoga (union with fruits) for the world. Rather, svabhāva — own-nature, structural propensity — operates. The verse is read across traditions as the foundation for the non-doership doctrine of 5.8-5.9. The engineering analog is structural-not-hero-narrative; the verse exceeds this.

positions.rendering: the embodied spirit, master of the city of his body
Advaita: the Self / Ātman, who does not create agency for the world; svabhāva = māyā/avidyā operates
Vishishtadvaita: the jīva established in its essential nature; svabhāva = the karmic conjunction operates

disagreements.explanation: Major sectarian disagreement. Shankara reads prabhu as the absolute Self (since later commentators in this lineage identify it with brahman); Ramanuja reads it as the individual jīva in its essential self-nature; some readings (e.g. some Madhva) read it as the Lord (Vishnu). All preserve the structural claim: agency-and-action-and-fruit are not authored by the prabhu but by svabhāva.

verses.sanskrit_devanagari:
नादत्ते कस्यचित्पापं न चैव सुकृतं विभुः ।
अज्ञानेनावृतं ज्ञानं तेन मुह्यन्ति जन्तवः ॥ १५ ॥
verses.sanskrit_iast:
nādatte kasyacit pāpaṁ na caiva sukṛtaṁ vibhuḥ |
ajñānenāvṛtaṁ jñānaṁ tena muhyanti jantavaḥ || 15 ||
verses.shankara_summary: Shankara: the vibhu (the all-pervading Self / brahman) does not take on anyone's sin or virtue. Knowledge (jñāna, the awareness of the Self) is veiled by ignorance (ajñāna / avidyā), and beings, deluded by this veiling, falsely attribute agency and karmic accumulation to the Self. Shankara's Advaita reading: the Self never accumulates karma; the appearance of karma-accumulation is a function of avidyā.
verses.ramanuja_summary: Ramanuja: the vibhu (the all-pervading, here read as the Lord or as the self in its essential nature, depending on context) does not take on anyone's pāpa or puṇya. Knowledge is veiled by ignorance, and beings are deluded — they attribute agency and fruit-coupling to the self that does not in essence have them. The verse establishes that fruit-of-action belongs to the karma-context (svabhāva), not to the self/Lord.
verses.literal_meaning: The all-pervading takes on neither anyone's sin nor anyone's virtue. Knowledge is veiled by ignorance; by this beings are deluded.
verses.traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna closes the metaphysical block. The vibhu (the all-pervading) does not take on anyone's pāpa (sin) or sukṛta (virtue). What appears as karma-accumulation is the veiling of jñāna by ajñāna — beings (jantavaḥ) are deluded by this veiling. The verse sets up the diagnosis that 5.16 will resolve: knowledge dispels the ignorance, and the diagnostic of mistaken agency dissolves.

positions.rendering: the Supreme Lord
the all-pervading Self / brahman; does not accumulate karma because it does not act
the all-pervading (Lord or self in essential nature); does not take on karma because karma belongs to the karmic conjunction

disagreements.explanation: Sectarian disagreement on the referent of vibhu. Shankara reads it as the Self/brahman; Ramanuja can read it as the Lord or the jīva-in-essential-nature; Prabhupada reads it as the Supreme Lord. All three preserve the structural claim: the vibhu does not take on pāpa/puṇya; what appears as karmic accumulation is a function of ignorance.
